title: Rebuilds Formal Developments
wikitext:
[]: Rebuild's Formal Developments.             Quote Post by LightDragonman » Fri Jan 24, 2014 5:45 pm Okay, I'll admit, this was inspired by the Driving Off The Map analysis of Metal Gear Solid 2, but I feel that can apply to the Rebuild saga's developments. Anyways, onto the topic at hand. As you may know, I have a whole bunch of grievances with 3.33. Mainly in that it seemed to completely undo development of the previous two films for the sake of cheap drama, left a whole bunch of unresolved threads that FINAL now has to at least address, placed Shinji in an un-winnable situation, etc. Needless to say, I think its narrative is quite messed up at this point. However, upon playing through Metal Gear Solid 2 again, and seeing how it played with the viewer's expectations and even twisted events seen in the first game in the second in order to throw the player off, I couldn't help but wonder if maybe 3.33's strengths lie not in its narrative developments, but in its formal developments. To quickly describe the term I'm using, Form is how the narrates through the story, expectations, visuals, and allusions. Metal Gear Solid 2 for example twisted its form to toy with the expectations of the players who where going in hoping for another experience like that seen in MGS1. Basically, it took their expectations for the sequel, and gave it to them, but done in a way that threw them off. It did this by subverting the actions seen in the first game, made the player go through recreations of the first game's events, but twisted and malformed, and ultimately led them to a very different conclusion than the first game's. In other words, by twisting and playing with the player's expectations and knowledge of the first game, 2 frustrated them, while also telling them to not let preconceived notions affect how we deal with new problems. Back to 3.33. Think back to the events and plot points seen in it. Most of what it contained mirrored events seen in the original series, and to a lesser extent the previous two films. However, all of it was twisted in some way so as to deny the same expected outcome that fans were familiar with and expected. Take Kaworu for example. His role in 3.33 mirrors his appearance in the original series. Like in the original, Kaworu appears to Shinji late in the story, and at a point where Shinji is at a low point mentally. His scene with them both playing piano together, him fixing his SDAT player, and even taking the bomb collar onto himself reflects the same sort of kindness seen by the original character's. His shock at finding out the angel he is looking for is not Adam, but Lilith, mirrors his horror at seeing what the Spears true purpose is. Finally, he ultimately dies at the end in order to save humanity, and ultimately leaves Shinji even more broken than he was before. However, this is when things start to break apart. In the original, Kaworu's act of kindness were arguably just a ruse in order to gain Shinji's trust so as to make killing him that much harder. His ultimate goal was not Shinji's happiness, but rather to trigger Third Impact. His death literally came at the hands of Shinji, in which he basically asked the latter to kill him. In 3.33 however, things are twisted around. Kaworu here genuinely wants Shinji to be happy, and aids him into going for the Spears, as opposed to Shinji trying to stop him from reaching Lilith in the original. It is he who at the end is trying to stop Shinji from ending the world, as opposed to the opposite in the original. Finally, his death, while happening via his own volition, fails to stop the chaos, instead having Mari be the one who ends it. Even his death is played with, with his head blowing up and the rest remaining intact, while the opposite happened in the original. I could post more examples, but you probably get the memo. What I'm basically saying here is that I feel that 3.33's strengths, and to that extent the strengths of the Rebuild saga as a whole, lie not in the narrative progress, but in the formal progress. 3.33 deliberately toyed with the events of the original so as to subvert the expectations of the viewer. With that in mind, I wouldn't be surprised if we see FINAL do more of the same. What say you guys? TL;DR: 3.33, like Metal Gear Solid 2, toys with the expectations of the viewers based on their pre-existing knowledge of the series so as to force them to think.

[]: Re: Rebuild's Formal Developments.             Quote Post by pwhodges » Fri Jan 24, 2014 6:12 pm LightDragonman wrote: 3.33 deliberately toyed with the events of the original so as to subvert the expectations of the viewer.

[]:             Quote Post by WunderBah » Fri Jan 24, 2014 6:25 pm You really like your MGS & NTE comparisons lol.

[]:             Quote Post by LightDragonman » Fri Jan 24, 2014 6:28 pm Is it overly obvious? But seriously, I feel that the two do share the same sort of formal techniques. Granted, a new viewer won't be able to catch onto them, but I feel that they are there for a reason.

[]: Re: Rebuild's Formal Developments.             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Fri Jan 24, 2014 10:30 pm LightDragonman wrote: Okay, I'll admit, this was inspired by the Driving Off The Map analysis of Metal Gear Solid 2

[]: Re: Rebuild's Formal Developments.             Quote Post by Jimbob » Fri Jan 24, 2014 10:36 pm pwhodges wrote: Can we assume that NTE is expected to be watched by people already familiar with the series? I think not; it is surely designed to stand alone, and be taken on its own merits.

[]: Re: Rebuild's Formal Developments.             Quote Post by TMBounty_Hunter » Sat Jan 25, 2014 11:01 am Jimbob wrote: In Japan anyway, and infact it being Eva might be a turn off to the uninitiated to Eva there because Eva is widely known throughout the whole nation as an Otaku thing. (ie, bad if you want to come accross as a social, well adjusted individual)

[]:             Quote Post by chee » Sat Jan 25, 2014 11:37 am Pretty much my thoughts as well.

[]:             Quote Post by LightDragonman » Mon Jan 27, 2014 12:59 am Yeah. I could go on with more of the formal comparisons and developments (3.33's opening fight mirroring both the Sandalphon and Arael fights in the original, Rei Q's entire character, Shinji's treatment, etc.) but I think that the point is pretty much clear to anyone who has seen the original. Now with all that said, I feel that FINAL will continue to do the same, and heck, looking at it from a more formal perspective kinda gives me a better prediction of what may happen. Looking back to 3.33's ending, Asuka dragging a despairing Shinji through the wasteland formally recalls Misato doing the same thing after saving him from the JSSDF in EoE. With that in mind, here's a bit of what I think will happen: Asuka's role will formally mirror Misato's in EoE. Not in terms of actually dying, but mainly in how she treats and tries to get Shinji to act. Heck, I wouldn't be surprised if she gives him the "adult kiss". It would satisfy the shippers at any rate. The fight against the MP-Evas, should they appear, will both resemble the original fight in EoE, and the JSSDF invasion of NERV. Considering that they are going to "where the Lilin" are, I bet that, if it is attacked, it will be a lot like the invasion in EoE. Rei Q's character will continue to mirror Rei III's, while at the same time, continuing the change seen in Rei II's character arc. Consider the ending of 3.33, where she was seen sadly looking at Shinji's SDAT player, a nod to the scene in 2.22. Heck, I would argue that her entire character began to formally recall Rei II ironically following Shinji's dismissal of her "not being Ayanami." Mainly, the whole beginning to act for herself, and showing sympathy towards Shinji. I bet she'll enter Unit 01 in a formal mirror to Rei III entering Lilith in EoE. Now obviously, I might be way off the ballpark here. Not to mention that it may not all work from a character growth POV, as some of you have pointed out. However, since I argue that Rebuild's story strengths lie not in the narrative, but the form, I feel that this is where FINAL may take the story. Feel free to debate though. I really want to know what you guys have to say on this.

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Mon Jan 27, 2014 5:59 am That's a very interesting analysis, it's clear that many of 3.0's scenes are meant to be bizarro dark reprises of scenes from the previous movies and the original series, another example is Misato removing her glasses leading to her introduction and first-name basis in a warm tone of voice vs Q!Misato removing her glasses(with the exact same gestures to boot!) leading to a declaration that he won't do anything anymore with an icy tone of voice and calling him on a full-name basis. As you said, Asuka dragging a catatonic Shinji through the ruins of Tokyo-3 points that her role in FINALE will be similar of Misato in EoE dragged a catatonic Shinji through a NERV HQ ruined by the JSSDF's assault, which the possibility that Asuka will also be the one trying to get him get a hold of himself and continue moving forward instead of succumbing to despair. Although it's possible that Anno will use a completely new narrative this time : Q was his way of completely put the previous narrative and parallels with the original series behind by having it having similarities with the lasts episodes and EoE : NERV HQ in ruins after what looks like a massive assault, the world completely changed by a Third Impact, Shinji losing his mind and triggering an Impact out of desperation, Kaworu sacrificing himself for Shinji and the Lilin. Some times ago on other threads it was theorized that on a narrative level, FINALE will be the first canon "post-EoE" story, since many elements from EoE were already referenced in Q. Even what looks like elements from EoE that will appear in FINALE could be interpreted differently : - Asuka will probably try to make Shinji get a hold of himself while they're stranded in the red desert, but she'll have probably others, more important things to do like piloting the Eva during the final battle, - the MP-EVAs will apparently be here, but in a very different form than the ones in EoE, almost the opposite in fact : whereas the Harpies where a small squad of nigh unkillable Evangelions, FINALE's MP-Evas seems to be an army of cheap Evagelions being torn apart by the dozen by a rampaging EVA-8+2 - while Shinji was essentially alone in his misery in EoE because everyone close to him was either dead, comatose or trying to prevent the end of the world, this time there's people who are around him and will potentially be able to help him : Asuka(while they're in the desert), Sakura(she's his assigned medical officer after all), and I wouldn't be surprised if Mari intervene too(she already showed glimpse of a warrior therapist behavior in 2.0 and 3.0), it's also possible that Kaji(if he's alive) give him a little "man to man" talk with some life lessons(a true father figure should be of big help to Shinji seeing how abyssal his true father was) - if the preview is to be believed, Shinji will found hope in FINAL, opposite to EoE where it was the last straw that made him go completely mad from despair and go on full omnicidal maniac mode. Well now that I think about it, it kinda correspond with what you talked about : 3.0 played with our expectations by being a dark inversion of 1.0, 2.0 and the TV Series first episodes(as well as an even worse version of episode 24 and some elements of EoE), so maybe FINALE will also play with our expectations by being also a mirror of EoE, meaning a lighter inversion of it by having everyone's situation get better, Shinji healing his mental scars in a less traumatic way than Instrumentality and mass tangifigation, and the good guy for once managing to completely defeat the bad guys and prevent the end of the world. Anno did said that the Rebuild's ending will be completely different than the original series and film after all.

[]:             Quote Post by Charsi » Mon Jan 27, 2014 10:44 am Wow... I never caught that Asuka dragging his ass through the desert mirrored Misato's in EoE.

[]:             Quote Post by LightDragonman » Tue Jan 28, 2014 12:10 am Heh, it was only after marathoning Metal Gear Solid 2 over and over that I began to pick up on the formal techniques used in 3.33. Further proof that NTE and MGS are basically the equivalents in their respective mediums. And yeah, I do see Asuka as formally taking the role Misato played in EoE. Heck, she will probably give Shinji the whole "So f***in what if I'm not you" speech, complete with the adult kiss right before she goes off to fight the MP-Evas or something. And the shippers would squeal in delight. I personally think however, that Rei Q's development is the most interesting to predict, especially when looking at it from a formal standpoint. Thinking back to her in 3.33, she was essentially the epitome of everything that the film did to deny both Shinji and the audience the expected outcome and development from 2.22's hope and optimism. Essentially a soulless shell, lacking any of the development and character depth that Rei II had gained, she could be seen as a reflection of how viewers saw the film for the first time. She simple goes through the motions, only doing what is needed of her, and is basically everything that Rei was initially hoped to be seen as by Anno. However, immediately starting after Shinji finally dismisses her as "not being Ayanami", 2.22's form slowly but surely begins to re-surface in her character. She shows genuine concern for Shinji, as noted by her addressing him as "Ikari-kun", just like how Rei II used to. Furthermore, her encounter with the massive Reilith head formally mirrors the shot after Unit 00 did its kamikaze attack on Zeruel. Heck, her being trapped inside Unit 09, unable to do anything and essentially helpless formally recalls Rei II when she was trapped inside Zeruel. Heck, both prisons even have a similar blueish color scheme (granted, it more resembles the color when Shinji pulled Rei back into Unit 01 but you get my drift)! Rei's ejection after being told by Asuka to "do what she wants to do" also formally calls back to when Shinji told Rei II that "there's no other Ayanami but you". Finally, in one of the biggest formal mirrors to 2.22, Rei Q, like Rei II before her, is seen looking at Shinji's discarded SDAT player. Note how this once again happened drink Shinji's lowest point. What does all of this mean? I'm not entirely positive on this, but I think that through the film's form, Anno is communicating to us that 2.22's ending and Rei II's development wasn't entirely all for naught, and that the hope and optimism seen in it is still there, albeit buried under all the unfortunate circumstances that happened because of it. Note also how all these formal callbacks to Rei II and 2.22's hope happens only after Shinji rejects Rei Q. I wonder if Anno predicted that we would have these sort of reactions upon seeing the film for the first time, and if like Shinji, we would be oblivious to the fact that not all hope from 2.22's ending was lost. How ironic is it, that Shinji began looking for redemption in all the wrong places by trying to return to happier times throughout the spears, when it is right there in Rei Q all along. Had he just stuck with her for a bit longer, he would have seen that, in a way, he did save Rei II. Now how will she progress in FINAL? Well from a formal standpoint, I think that Rei Q now has the ability to both redeem the failures made from both 2.22's ending and 3.33's actions, while at the same time, be the one who shows that not all was lost from the former's finale. Heck, I bet that she'll enter Unit 01 in a formal mirror to Rei III entering Lilith in EoE, only here it's to give Shinji hope as opposed to trying to rebel against Gendo. Whatever happens, I am most excited to see how she develops. Feel free to debate of course. TL;DR: Rei Q, imo, subtly recalled Rei II's character, and for that matter, 2.22's developments, in a formal manner, maybe as to both give a hint as to how she'll play out, and maybe as a way to tell us that not all was lost at the end of 2.22.

[]:             Quote Post by AngelNo13Bardiel » Tue Jan 28, 2014 8:31 am LightDragonman wrote: And the shippers would squeal in delight.

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Tue Jan 28, 2014 9:00 am LightDragonman wrote: And yeah, I do see Asuka as formally taking the role Misato played in EoE. Heck, she will probably give Shinji the whole "So f***in what if I'm not you" speech, complete with the adult kiss right before she goes off to fight the MP-Evas or something. And the shippers would squeal in delight.

[]:             Quote Post by LightDragonman » Tue Jan 28, 2014 10:48 pm As for Rei Q being a formal way to tell us that 2.0's ending and Rei II's development wasn't entirely for naught, what you mean is that since Rei Q is a clone of Yui like Rei II, then if Rei II was a human being capable of emotions and growth, then Rei Q must also be considered a human being and not a soulless and emotionless husk, right? Because for me it's the only way the two character development can be tied, since Rei II and Rei Q are fundamentally two different characters.

[]:             Quote Post by Yaywalter » Wed Jan 29, 2014 5:37 am While reading this thread, a horrific idea popped into my head... What if the "adult kiss" comes from Kaji? I mean... I can't help but feel that if Kaji's still alive (which I think he is) then he'll die in FINAL, almost certainly by getting shot. Maybe even by Misato, y'know, just to fuck with the fans. And it would make sense if Kaji is with Team NERV.

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Wed Jan 29, 2014 5:50 am LightDragonman wrote: Kind of. However, thinking back to it, Rei II and Rei Q are essentially the same sort of relationship with Rei and Lilith in the original series. The difference being that in the original the body was hung up, while the soul was in the active one. Here, it's the reverse, as Rei II takes over Lilith's placement, with Rei Q obviously being Rei III. Heck, she formally recalls the third Rei's doubts and the like when she returned to her apartment.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Wed Jan 29, 2014 4:59 pm Yaywalter wrote: And it would make sense if Kaji is with Team NERV.

[]:             Quote Post by LightDragonman » Wed Jan 29, 2014 10:33 pm Yes, she has doubts about herself like Rei III had, but the nature of the doubts are different : Rei III was sure that she was a "resurrection" of Rei II because she carries the same soul(Lilith's), what she doubted was her feelings toward Gendo and Shinji, more precisely who she put above the other in her heart, especially since it seems clear that Gendo only see her as a tool to reunite with Yui, and she took her decision in EoE when she ditched Gendo for Shinji. Rei Q on the other hand has a pure identity crisis : at first she's sure that she's Rei Ayanami, since that what the Commander told her and later Shinji calls her by that name, but then he starts to asks her questions about Rei Ayanami that Rei Q don't know, then claim that she's not Rei Ayanami, and all the other pilots she met concur, the crisis culminating when the 12th Angel absorbs Lilith's remains and morphes insto a giant head with Rei's face.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Wed Jan 29, 2014 11:17 pm Sailor Star Dust wrote: Wasn't there some implication that the groundwork for Wille started out in 2.0 thanks to Kaji and Mari? Or was that just fan speculation?

[]: Rebuild's Formal Developments.             Quote Post by LightDragonman » Fri Jan 24, 2014 5:45 pm Okay, I'll admit, this was inspired by the Driving Off The Map analysis of Metal Gear Solid 2, but I feel that can apply to the Rebuild saga's developments. Anyways, onto the topic at hand. As you may know, I have a whole bunch of grievances with 3.33. Mainly in that it seemed to completely undo development of the previous two films for the sake of cheap drama, left a whole bunch of unresolved threads that FINAL now has to at least address, placed Shinji in an un-winnable situation, etc. Needless to say, I think its narrative is quite messed up at this point. However, upon playing through Metal Gear Solid 2 again, and seeing how it played with the viewer's expectations and even twisted events seen in the first game in the second in order to throw the player off, I couldn't help but wonder if maybe 3.33's strengths lie not in its narrative developments, but in its formal developments. To quickly describe the term I'm using, Form is how the narrates through the story, expectations, visuals, and allusions. Metal Gear Solid 2 for example twisted its form to toy with the expectations of the players who where going in hoping for another experience like that seen in MGS1. Basically, it took their expectations for the sequel, and gave it to them, but done in a way that threw them off. It did this by subverting the actions seen in the first game, made the player go through recreations of the first game's events, but twisted and malformed, and ultimately led them to a very different conclusion than the first game's. In other words, by twisting and playing with the player's expectations and knowledge of the first game, 2 frustrated them, while also telling them to not let preconceived notions affect how we deal with new problems. Back to 3.33. Think back to the events and plot points seen in it. Most of what it contained mirrored events seen in the original series, and to a lesser extent the previous two films. However, all of it was twisted in some way so as to deny the same expected outcome that fans were familiar with and expected. Take Kaworu for example. His role in 3.33 mirrors his appearance in the original series. Like in the original, Kaworu appears to Shinji

His shock at finding out the angel he is looking for is not Adam, but Lilith, mirrors his horror at seeing what the Spears true purpose is. Finally, he ultimately dies at the end in order to save humanity, and ultimately leaves Shinji even more broken than he was before. However, this is when things start to break apart. In the original, Kaworu's act of kindness were arguably just a ruse in order to gain Shinji's trust so as to make killing him that much harder. His ultimate goal was not Shinji's happiness, but rather to trigger Third Impact. His death literally came at the hands of Shinji, in which he basically asked the latter to kill him. In 3.33 however, things are twisted around. Kaworu here genuinely wants Shinji to be happy, and aids him into going for the Spears, as opposed to Shinji trying to stop him from reaching Lilith in the original. It is he who at the end is trying to stop Shinji from ending the world, as opposed to the opposite in the original. Finally, his death, while happening via his own volition, fails to stop the chaos, instead having Mari be the one who ends it. Even his death is played with, with his head blowing up and the rest remaining intact, while the opposite happened in the original. I could post more examples, but you probably get the memo. Article: Rebuilds Formal Developments
 narrative level, FINALE will be the first canon "post-EoE" story, since many elements from EoE were already referenced in Q. Even what looks like elements from EoE that will appear in FINALE could be interpreted differently : - Asuka will probably try to make Shinji get a hold of himself while they're stranded in the red desert, but she'll have probably others, more important things to do like piloting the Eva during the final battle, - the MP-EVAs will apparently be here, but in a very different form than the ones in EoE, almost the opposite in fact : whereas the Harpies where a small squad of nigh unkillable Evangelions, FINALE's MP-Evas seems to be an army of cheap Evagelions being torn apart by the dozen by a rampaging EVA-8+2 - while Shinji was essentially alone in his misery in EoE because everyone close to him was either dead, comatose or trying to prevent the end of the world, this time there's people who are around him and will potentially be able to help him : Asuka(while they're in the desert), Sakura(she's his assigned medical officer after all), and I wouldn't be surprised if Mari intervene too(she already showed glimpse of a warrior therapist behavior in 2.0 and 3.0), it's also possible that Kaji(if he's alive) give him a little "man to man" talk with some life lessons(a true father figure should be of big help to Shinji seeing how abyssal his true father was) - if the preview is to be believed, Shinji will found hope in FINAL, opposite to EoE where it was the last straw that made him go completely mad from despair and go on full omnicidal maniac mode. Well now that I think about it, it kinda correspond with what you talked about : 3.0 played with our expectations by being a dark inversion of 1.0, 2.0 and the TV Series first episodes(as well as an even worse version of episode 24 and some elements of EoE), so maybe FINALE will also play with our expectations by being also a mirror of EoE, meaning a lighter inversion of it by having everyone's situation get better, Shinji healing his mental scars in a less traumatic way than Instrumentality and mass tangifigation, and the good guy for once managing to completely defeat the bad guys and prevent

Article: Rebuilds Formal Developments
, meaning a lighter inversion of it by having everyone's situation get better, Shinji healing his mental scars in a less traumatic way than Instrumentality and mass tangifigation, and the good guy for once managing to completely defeat the bad guys and prevent the end of the world. Anno did said that the Rebuild's ending will be completely different than the original series and film after all.

[]:             Quote Post by Charsi » Mon Jan 27, 2014 10:44 am Wow... I never caught that Asuka dragging his ass through the desert mirrored Misato's in EoE.

[]:             Quote Post by LightDragonman » Tue Jan 28, 2014 12:10 am Heh, it was only after marathoning Metal Gear Solid 2 over and over that I began to pick up on the formal techniques used in 3.33. Further proof that NTE and MGS are basically the equivalents in their respective mediums. And yeah, I do see Asuka as formally taking the role Misato played in EoE. Heck, she will probably give Shinji the whole "So f***in what if I'm not you" speech, complete with the adult kiss right before she goes off to fight the MP-Evas or something. And the shippers would squeal in delight. I personally think however, that Rei Q's development is the most interesting to predict, especially when looking at it from a formal standpoint. Thinking back to her in 3.33, she was essentially the epitome of everything that the film did to deny both Shinji and the audience the expected outcome and development from 2.22's hope and optimism. Essentially a soulless shell, lacking any of the development and character depth that Rei II had gained, she could be seen as a reflection of how viewers saw the film for the first time. She simple goes through the motions, only doing what is needed of her, and is basically everything that Rei was initially hoped to be seen as by Anno. However, immediately starting after Shinji finally dismisses her as "not being Ayanami", 2.22's form slowly but surely begins to re-surface in her character. She shows genuine concern for Shinji, as noted by her addressing him as "Ikari-kun", just like how Rei II used to. Furthermore, her encounter with the massive Reilith head formally mirrors the shot after Unit 00 did its kamikaze attack

Article: Rebuilds Formal Developments
 for Shinji, as noted by her addressing him as "Ikari-kun", just like how Rei II used to. Furthermore, her encounter with the massive Reilith head formally mirrors the shot after Unit 00 did its kamikaze attack on Zeruel. Heck, her being trapped inside Unit 09, unable to do anything and essentially helpless formally recalls Rei II when she was trapped inside Zeruel. Heck, both prisons even have a similar blueish color scheme (granted, it more resembles the color when Shinji pulled Rei back into Unit 01 but you get my drift)! Rei's ejection after being told by Asuka to "do what she wants to do" also formally calls back to when Shinji told Rei II that "there's no other Ayanami but you". Finally, in one of the biggest formal mirrors to 2.22, Rei Q, like Rei II before her, is seen looking at Shinji's discarded SDAT player. Note how this once again happened drink Shinji's lowest point. What does all of this mean? I'm not entirely positive on this, but I think that through the film's form, Anno is communicating to us that 2.22's ending and Rei II's development wasn't entirely all for naught, and that the hope and optimism seen in it is still there, albeit buried under all the unfortunate circumstances that happened because of it. Note also how all these formal callbacks to Rei II and 2.22's hope happens only after Shinji rejects Rei Q. I wonder if Anno predicted that we would have these sort of reactions upon seeing the film for the first time, and if like Shinji, we would be oblivious to the fact that not all hope from 2.22's ending was lost. How ironic is it, that Shinji began looking for redemption in all the wrong places by trying to return to happier times throughout the spears, when it is right there in Rei Q all along. Had he just stuck with her for a bit longer, he would have seen that, in a way, he did save Rei II. Now how will she progress in FINAL? Well from a formal standpoint, I think that Rei Q now has the ability to both redeem the failures made from both 2.22's ending and 3.33's actions, while at the same time, be the one who shows that not all was lost from the former's finale. Heck, I bet that she'll enter Unit 01 in

Article: Rebuilds Formal Developments
 from both 2.22's ending and 3.33's actions, while at the same time, be the one who shows that not all was lost from the former's finale. Heck, I bet that she'll enter Unit 01 in a formal mirror to Rei III entering Lilith in EoE, only here it's to give Shinji hope as opposed to trying to rebel against Gendo. Whatever happens, I am most excited to see how she develops. Feel free to debate of course. TL;DR: Rei Q, imo, subtly recalled Rei II's character, and for that matter, 2.22's developments, in a formal manner, maybe as to both give a hint as to how she'll play out, and maybe as a way to tell us that not all was lost at the end of 2.22.

[]:             Quote Post by LightDragonman » Wed Jan 29, 2014 10:33 pm Yes, she has doubts about herself like Rei III had, but the nature of the doubts are different : Rei III was sure that she was a "resurrection" of Rei II because she carries the same soul(Lilith's), what she doubted was her feelings toward Gendo and Shinji, more precisely who she put above the other in her heart, especially since it seems clear that Gendo only see her as a tool to reunite with Yui, and she took her decision in EoE when she ditched Gendo for Shinji. Rei Q on the other hand has a pure identity crisis : at first she's sure that she's Rei Ayanami, since that what the Commander told her and later Shinji calls her by that name, but then he starts to asks her questions about Rei Ayanami that Rei Q don't know, then claim that she's not Rei Ayanami, and all the other pilots she
